heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Fox and Geese Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: Fox and Geese is a medieval hunt game played on a cross-shaped board of 33 points — the same shape as a peg solitaire board. One player controls a single fox, the other commands 13 geese, and the asymmetric struggle makes every game tense. Building the board from plywood and painting it is a satisfying afternoon project that connects kids to games monks played in cathedral cloisters.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: 9×9 inch birch plywood, ¼ inch thick (available at Hobby Lobby or Home Depot)|Acrylic paint — 2 colors (e.g. dark brown and cream), from Michaels|Clear water-based polyurethane spray, Rust-Oleum matte|Fine-tip permanent marker or wood-burning pen|Ruler and pencil|Sandpaper — 120 grit and 220 grit sheets|14 wooden craft discs, ¾ inch diameter (13 geese + 1 fox): Michaels SKU or similar|Acrylic paint in two contrasting colors for pieces|Optional: ¼ inch wooden dowel scraps to make peg-style pieces instead of discs
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the plywood smooth with 120-grit paper, then finish with 220-grit. Wipe off dust with a dry cloth.|2. Paint the entire board surface with cream or light-tan acrylic paint. Let dry 30 minutes. Apply a second coat if needed.|3. Mark the board grid: the cross shape is 7 columns × 7 rows of points, but only the cross-shaped portion is used. Using a ruler, lightly pencil a 7×7 grid of dots spaced 1¼ inches apart (total playing area about 7½×7½ inches, centered on the board).|4. Erase the 4 corner squares of dots (each corner block is a 2×2 group of 4 points). You should have exactly 33 points remaining in a cross shape.|5. Using a fine-tip permanent marker or pencil, draw the horizontal and vertical lines connecting all adjacent points. Then add diagonal lines in the four arms of the cross (diagonals connect points only within each arm, not across the center).|6. Go over all lines neatly with a dark brown acrylic paint and a thin brush, or use a wood-burning pen for a classic look. Let dry completely.|7. Apply two coats of matte polyurethane spray to seal the board. Let cure 2 hours between coats.|8. Paint 13 craft discs white (geese) and 1 disc red or orange (fox). Let dry. Apply a clear coat.|9. To set up: place the 13 geese on all points in the bottom three rows (rows 5, 6, 7 of the cross). The fox starts on any empty point in the upper portion — traditionally the center of the board.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Adult supervision required when using a wood-burning pen — it reaches temperatures over 700°F and can cause serious burns. Polyurethane spray should be used in a well-ventilated area or outdoors. Keep spray away from open flames.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Fox and Geese boards have been found carved into the stone floors of medieval cathedrals — monks apparently played between prayers. The game is asymmetric: the fox wins by capturing enough geese that they cannot trap it (usually 6 or more), while the geese win by surrounding the fox so it cannot move. For a more durable build, use ¾ inch pine instead of plywood and drill shallow ¼ inch holes for peg-style pieces cut from ¼ inch dowel. You can customize the board by adding Celtic knotwork borders with a wood-burning pen.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Walnut or cherry hardwood, ¾ inch thick, hand-oiled with Danish oil or tung oil. Playing surface may be inlaid with a contrasting maple or ash panel for the cross-board grid area.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 10×10 inches ± ¼ inch. Playing grid: 7×7 points at 1¼ inch spacing (7½ inch square grid), centered on board with 1¼ inch border. Board thickness: ¾ inch. 33 shallow drilled point-holes, ¼ inch diameter × ⅛ inch deep.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Grain raised and sanded to 320 grit. Two coats hand-rubbed Danish oil, buffed between coats. Lines burned or laser-engraved — no paint on playing surface. Optional: chamfered or rounded edges, ⅛ inch radius.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 14 turned wooden pegs: 13 geese in maple or light ash (¼ inch diameter × ¾ inch tall), 1 fox in dark walnut or hand-painted red (same dimensions). Alternatively: hand-turned mushroom-cap pawns, ½ inch base diameter.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Nine Men's Morris Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: Nine Men's Morris is one of the oldest strategy games in the world — boards have been carved into the benches of Westminster Abbey and Egyptian temple floors. Three nested squares connected by lines create 24 intersection points where players battle to form 'mills' of three in a row. This board is beginner-friendly to build: just squares within squares and a few connecting lines.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 10×10 inch birch plywood square, ¼ inch thick (Home Depot or Hobby Lobby)|Acrylic paint — dark brown or black for lines, light tan or cream for background (Michaels)|Matte polyurethane spray sealant|Ruler, pencil, fine-tip brush|Sandpaper — 120 grit and 220 grit|18 wooden craft discs, ¾ inch diameter — 9 each in 2 colors (Michaels)|Optional: wood-burning pen for engraved lines
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the plywood with 120-grit, then 220-grit. Wipe clean.|2. Paint the entire surface with light tan acrylic. Let dry fully, then apply a second coat.|3. Measure and lightly pencil the three concentric squares: Outer square — 8 inches × 8 inches, centered on board (1 inch border all around). Middle square — 5 inches × 5 inches, centered. Inner square — 2 inches × 2 inches, centered.|4. Mark the 24 intersection points: 4 corners of each square (12 points) + midpoint of each side of each square (12 points). Each square has 8 points for a total of 24.|5. Draw the three squares with a ruler and pencil.|6. Draw the 6 connector lines: one line from the midpoint of each outer square side to the midpoint of the corresponding inner square side, passing through the midpoint of the middle square. These 6 lines (2 horizontal, 2 vertical — but leave the center of the inner square blank).|7. Go over all lines and points with dark brown paint and a fine brush, or use a wood-burning pen. Make each point dot about ⅛ inch diameter.|8. Seal with two coats of matte polyurethane spray. Let cure.|9. Paint 9 craft discs one color (e.g. red) and 9 another color (e.g. black). Let dry, seal with clear coat.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: If using a wood-burning pen, adult must operate it — tip is extremely hot. Work on a fireproof surface. Spray sealant in well-ventilated area only.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Nine Men's Morris boards have been carved into medieval cathedral stonework and found in ancient Egypt — making this one of history's most widely played games. Shakespeare mentions it by name in A Midsummer Night's Dream. The game has three phases: placing pieces, moving pieces, and the endgame 'flying' phase when a player is down to 3 pieces and can jump anywhere. For a premium look, use contrasting wood stain instead of paint for the squares, or engrave the lines with a wood-burning pen and fill with dark wax.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Walnut or cherry hardwood, ¾ inch thick, finished with hand-rubbed tung oil. Optional inlay: maple strips set into routed channels for the lines, creating a flush two-tone surface.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 10×10 inches ± ¼ inch. Outer square: 8×8 inches. Middle square: 5×5 inches. Inner square: 2×2 inches. All squares centered. 24 point positions marked with shallow drilled dimples (⅛ inch diameter × 1/16 inch deep) or brass inlay dots.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Sanded to 320 grit. Hand-rubbed oil finish, 3 coats. Lines routed ⅛ inch wide × 1/16 inch deep and filled with contrasting dark epoxy or wood filler, sanded flush. Optional: branded/laser-engraved maker's mark on underside.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 18 turned wooden playing pieces: 9 each in two contrasting woods (e.g., walnut and maple). Mushroom-cap pawns, ¾ inch base diameter, ½ inch tall. Or hand-turned discs with chamfered edges.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Millionaire's Perplexity Puzzle Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: The Millionaire's Perplexity is a Victorian mathematical puzzle from Dudeney's 1917 Amusements in Mathematics: distribute exactly $1,000,000 using only powers of 7, with no denomination used more than 6 times. Building a tactile token-and-board set makes this abstract puzzle into a hands-on family activity. The finished artifact is a beautifully labeled tray with denomination tokens — elegant and reusable.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 8×5 inch wooden craft plaque or thin pine board, ¼–½ inch thick (Michaels or Hobby Lobby)|Balsa wood sheet for token tray dividers, ⅛ inch thick (Hobby Lobby)|Acrylic paint — gold and dark green (Michaels)|Fine-tip permanent marker or letter stamps for labeling|7 denominations of cardboard tokens (cut from thick cardstock): label $1, $7, $49, $343, $2401, $16807, $117649 — 7 of each|Ruler, pencil, craft knife or scissors|Glue gun or wood glue|Clear acrylic sealer spray
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Paint the wooden plaque dark green as a base color. Let dry completely.|2. Paint a gold border around the edge with a thin brush.|3. Using a ruler and pencil, divide the plaque surface into 7 labeled denomination zones, each about 1×4 inches. Label each zone with one power of 7: $1 | $7 | $49 | $343 | $2,401 | $16,807 | $117,649.|4. Use a fine-tip permanent marker or letter stamps to add labels. Optionally, paint them in gold letters.|5. Seal the plaque with two coats of clear acrylic spray. Let cure.|6. Cut 49 square tokens (7 per denomination) from thick cardstock, each 1×1 inch.|7. Label each token with its denomination value using a marker.|8. Optional: glue balsa wood strips inside a small box lid to create a divided token storage tray matching the denominations.|9. Write the puzzle challenge on a card: 'Using only powers of 7 (and no denomination more than 6 times), make exactly $1,000,000.'
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Craft knife use requires adult supervision. Keep fingers clear of the blade and always cut away from the body on a cutting mat. Spray sealant must be used outdoors or in a well-ventilated area.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: This puzzle works because any number can be expressed in base 7 — the constraint of 'no denomination more than 6 times' mirrors the digits 0-6 in base-7 notation. The answer: 1,000,000 in base 7 is 11,333,311, meaning you need 2×$117,649 + 1×$16,807 + 3×$2,401 + 3×$343 + 3×$49 + 1×$7 + 1×$1. This makes a beautiful STEM conversation piece. The board can be repurposed for other base-conversion puzzles by changing the labels.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Dark-stained walnut or mahogany presentation board, ½ inch thick. Denomination zones inlaid or laser-engraved. Token set in turned brass or painted hardwood discs.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 10×6 inches ± ¼ inch. Seven denomination zones each 1×4 inches, separated by ⅛ inch routed dividing lines. Storage drawer or fitted box: 4×3 inches.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Sanded to 320 grit, stained dark walnut, hand-rubbed with paste wax. Denomination labels laser-engraved and filled with gold paint. Edges chamfered.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 49 tokens in 7 groups of 7: laser-engraved or stamped wooden discs, 1 inch diameter, ¼ inch thick. Each denomination group in a distinct wood (or painted in gilt on dark base). Stored in a fitted sliding-lid box.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Buried Treasure Fraction Puzzle Set
heritage_skills_entry.summary: The Buried Treasure puzzle from Dudeney's Amusements in Mathematics presents a fractional division challenge: three explorers share treasure based on the proportion of supplies each contributed. Building a physical token-and-card set turns this Victorian arithmetic puzzle into a hands-on family math game. The finished kit is a portable treasure-themed puzzle box perfect for rainy days.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 6×4 inch wooden craft box or cigar box (Michaels or Hobby Lobby)|Cardstock or thick paper for puzzle cards|Gold spray paint or gold acrylic paint (Michaels)|100 brass-colored craft beads or gold tokens (Hobby Lobby) — to represent treasure shares|Ruler, pencil, fine-tip marker|Scissors or craft knife|Decoupage glue (Mod Podge) for decorating box|Optional: printable 'treasure map' for box lid decoration|Clear acrylic sealer spray
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Decorate the box lid with a printed or hand-drawn treasure map motif. Apply with decoupage glue.|2. Paint interior of box and token tray with gold acrylic paint. Let dry.|3. Create three 'Explorer Cards' on cardstock, each listing one explorer's supply contribution: Explorer A — contributed 2/3 of supplies | Explorer B — contributed 1/4 of supplies | Explorer C — contributed the remainder (1 − 2/3 − 1/4 = 1/12).|4. Create a 'Treasure Card' listing the total treasure: $1,000 (or use 100 tokens to represent $1,000 in units of $10).|5. Create a 'Solution Card' showing the proportional shares: A receives $666.67, B receives $250, C receives $83.33. Store face-down until players have worked out their answers.|6. Add 100 gold tokens to the box for hands-on calculation.|7. Create a 'Puzzle Challenge' card with the problem statement and instructions: 'Divide the treasure proportionally based on each explorer's supply contribution. Use the tokens to show your work.'|8. Seal all cards with a light coat of clear spray. Store everything in the decorated box.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Craft knife use requires adult supervision. Spray sealant and spray paint must be used outdoors or in well-ventilated area. Keep paint and solvents away from food.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Proportional division of shared finds was a genuine legal matter in Victorian maritime law — treasure disputes required exactly this arithmetic. The puzzle neatly illustrates why common denominators matter: finding the LCD of 3 and 4 is 12, making A's share 8/12, B's 3/12, and C's 1/12. The token set can be reused for any fraction puzzle. For older kids, extend the challenge: 'What if D contributed $50 in cash — how does that change each share?'

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Hand-stained oak or cherry presentation box with sliding or hinged lid, ¼ inch thick sides. Interior compartment with 3 labeled sections. 100 brass token coins, custom-stamped.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Box: 7×5×2 inches ± ¼ inch. Interior divided into 4 sections: 3 explorer compartments + 1 token compartment. Explorer cards: standard 3×5 inches. Tokens: ¾ inch diameter brass discs.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Box exterior stained dark walnut, waxed finish. Interior lined with burgundy felt. Laser-engraved puzzle text on lid or inside of lid. Tokens: antique brass finish, custom-stamped denomination.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 100 brass-finish metal or turned-wood token coins, ¾ inch diameter. 4 laminated puzzle cards in a kraft-card sleeve. Optional: a brass 'X marks the spot' inlay on the lid.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build The Miller's Puzzle Token Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: From Dudeney's Canterbury Puzzles, the Miller's Puzzle arranges nine numbered tokens in a specific pattern where pairs multiplied by their single neighbors must equal the central number. Building a physical token board makes the abstract puzzle tangible and reusable — it's a beautiful introduction to multiplicative thinking for ages 10 and up.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 8×5 inch wooden craft plaque or thin pine board (Michaels or Hobby Lobby)|9 large wooden craft coins or discs, 1½ inch diameter (Hobby Lobby or Michaels)|Acrylic paint — tan/cream for board, dark stain for tokens|Fine-tip permanent marker or number stencils|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|Clear polyurethane spray|Wood glue (optional, for layout template)|Pencil and ruler
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Paint with cream or tan acrylic as base. Let dry.|2. Lightly sketch the token layout on the board: the arrangement is a horizontal row of 5 positions (single, pair, triple, pair, single) — positions labeled 1 through 9 left to right. The full puzzle layout shows: [1 sack] [2 sacks] [3 sacks in center] [2 sacks] [1 sack], where the center group is the 3-digit product and the outer groups multiply to match it.|3. Mark 9 circles on the board at the token positions using a coin as a template. Space them about ¾ inch apart.|4. Paint the token position circles in a darker shade so they're clearly visible as 'slots.'|5. Seal the board with polyurethane spray.|6. Paint or stain the 9 wooden discs. Let dry.|7. Number the discs 1 through 9 using a permanent marker or stencil.|8. The starting configuration is: [7] [28] [196] [34] [5] — where 7×28=196 but 5×34≠196. The puzzle: rearrange discs so BOTH pairs × singles equal the center.|9. Write the puzzle rules and the solution hint on a small card tucked under the board.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Polyurethane spray must be used outdoors or with strong ventilation. Sandpaper creates fine dust — wear a dust mask when sanding, especially children.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: The solution to the Miller's Puzzle requires finding that the center number must be a perfect square (196 = 14²). The valid arrangement is [9] [28] [196] [47] — wait, there are actually multiple solutions: one minimum-move solution exists where only a few sacks swap. Encourage kids to find ALL solutions, not just one. The puzzle demonstrates that multiplication constraints are much harder than additive ones — a key insight in number theory. The physical token board can be repurposed for any multiplication-arrangement puzzle.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Cherry or maple hardwood presentation board, ½ inch thick. Nine turned hardwood token discs in a contrasting wood. Board with laser-engraved slot positions and decorative Canterbury Tales-style border.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 12×5 inches ± ¼ inch. Nine token positions in a linear arrangement, each 1½ inch diameter circle. Center three positions slightly inset or elevated. Token discs: 1½ inch diameter × ⅜ inch thick.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Board sanded to 320 grit, hand-rubbed tung oil finish. Token position circles laser-engraved or routed ⅛ inch deep. Numerals 1-9 engraved on tokens, filled with black or gold wax. Edges rounded with ⅛ inch router roundover bit.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 9 turned hardwood discs — alternating walnut and maple for visual distinction. Numbers laser-engraved and filled with contrasting color wax. Stored in a small felt-lined tray at board rear.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build The Knight's Puzzle Grid Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: The Knight's Puzzle from Canterbury Puzzles asks: how many squares of all sizes and orientations (including tilted ones) can be formed on a grid of points on a heraldic shield? Building the grid as a peg board makes the puzzle hands-on — players use string or rubber bands to form and count squares, discovering how many they missed by thinking only of straight-aligned squares.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 10×10 inch pine board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Small nails or panel pins, ¾ inch long — about 80 (Home Depot hardware aisle)|Small rubber bands or string for marking squares|Ruler and pencil|Hammer|Sandpaper — 120 grit|Dark wood stain or paint (Michaels)|Polyurethane sealant spray|Optional: graph paper for planning nail layout
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Apply dark stain or paint and let dry.|2. Mark a 9×9 grid of nail positions on the board surface (81 points), spaced 1 inch apart. The grid should be centered, leaving a ½ inch border. Mark all positions lightly in pencil first.|3. Hammer a small nail or panel pin into each of the 81 marked positions. Drive nails in about ½ inch deep so they stand upright and firm, with about ¼ inch protruding.|4. Seal the board (around the nails) with polyurethane spray if desired.|5. Cut rubber bands or prepare lengths of string to form squares on the peg grid.|6. Write the puzzle challenge on an accompanying card: 'How many squares of ANY size and orientation — including tilted squares — can be formed by choosing 4 nails as corners?' (Answer: 105 for a 4×4 grid subset; varies by grid size.)|7. Include a solution envelope with the count broken down by size and tilt angle.|8. Optional: mark the pegs at the heraldic shield boundary positions with a small dab of colored paint.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Adult supervision required when hammering nails, especially for younger children. Nails have sharp points — keep them pointed away from hands. Wear safety glasses when hammering.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: The key insight of this puzzle is that tilted squares count — a square rotated 45° whose corners land on grid points is a valid square. For an n×n grid of points, the total count of all squares (including tilted) follows a specific formula involving sums of squares. Most people drastically undercount by ignoring the tilted ones. The peg board is also perfect for dozens of other geometry puzzles: triangles, rectangles, polygons. It makes an excellent permanent math manipulative for the family.

fabrica_spec.commission_title: The Knight's Puzzle Grid Board — Artisan Commission
fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Dark walnut or ebonized oak board, ¾ inch thick. Brass nails or brass rod pegs set into pre-drilled holes. Optional: heraldic shield shape routed from board edges.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 12×12 inches ± ¼ inch. Peg grid: 9×9 array, 1 inch spacing, centered with 1½ inch border. Pegs: ⅛ inch diameter brass rod, ⅜ inch tall above surface. Holes drilled ⅜ inch deep.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Board ebonized or dark-stained, sanded to 320 grit, paste wax finish. Brass pegs polished and press-fit into holes. Optional laser-engraved border with Canterbury Tales heraldic motif.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 81 brass rod pegs (⅛ inch diameter). Supplied with 12 colored rubber bands and a solution card in a paper envelope. Optional: silk-screened coordinate labels (A-I, 1-9) along board edges.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: The Mutilated Chessboard is one of mathematics' most elegant impossibility proofs: remove two diagonally opposite corner squares from a standard 8×8 chessboard — can you tile the remaining 62 squares with 31 dominoes? Building the board and a set of wooden dominoes lets the whole family struggle with the puzzle before the beautiful coloring argument reveals why it's impossible.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 12×12 inch birch plywood sheet, ¼ inch thick (Home Depot)|Two contrasting acrylic paint colors — black and cream (Michaels)|31 wooden dominoes: cut from ¼ inch plywood scrap, each 2×1 inch (or purchase a standard double-six domino set at Hobby Lobby and ignore pips)|Ruler, pencil, fine-tip marker|Jigsaw or hand saw for cutting dominoes|Sandpaper — 120 grit and 220 grit|Tape measure|Matte polyurethane spray sealant
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the plywood sheet smooth. Mark an 8×8 grid of 1½-inch squares, centered (12×12 board, squares 1½ inch each, total playing area 12 inches). Use ruler and pencil — mark all 9 horizontal lines and 9 vertical lines.|2. Paint alternating squares black and cream to create the chessboard pattern. Work in rows, checking the alternating pattern carefully. Let dry between colors.|3. The two squares to remove: top-left corner (a8) and bottom-right corner (h1) — both are the same color (both light or both dark). Mark these two squares with an 'X' or simply paint them a different color (e.g., red) to show they are 'removed.'|4. Seal the board with two coats of matte polyurethane. Let cure.|5. Cut 31 dominoes from ¼ inch plywood scraps: each domino is 3×1½ inches (covering exactly 2 squares of the board). Sand all edges smooth.|6. Optionally paint dominoes in a contrasting color (e.g., natural wood with black border).|7. Write the puzzle challenge on a card: 'Can you cover all 62 remaining squares with the 31 dominoes? Each domino covers exactly 2 adjacent squares.' Include the solution envelope with the proof.|8. Challenge family members to try for 10 minutes before revealing the elegant proof: both removed squares are the same color, so 32 squares of one color and 30 of the other remain — but every domino must cover one of each color, making it impossible.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Adult must operate the jigsaw for cutting domino pieces. Clamp the plywood securely before cutting. Wear safety glasses. Polyurethane spray in ventilated area only.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: This puzzle introduces 'proof by coloring' — a technique used throughout combinatorics and computer science. The coloring argument is one of the most beautiful in mathematics: regardless of how you try to arrange the dominoes, you always run out of one color. This same reasoning proves impossibility in many tiling and covering problems. The puzzle was popularized by mathematician Martin Gardner and has been used in university logic courses ever since. Build the board in walnut and maple for a heirloom version.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Alternating walnut and maple inlay squares, ¾ inch thick hardwood border frame. Two corner squares either missing (routed out) or marked with recessed brass X inlays. 31 two-tone wood dominoes.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 14×14 inches total. Playing grid: 8×8 squares at 1½ inch each (12×12 inches). Border: 1 inch walnut frame. Thickness: ¾ inch. Dominoes: 3×1½×⅜ inches each.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Walnut and maple squares hand-fitted and glued, sanded flush to 320 grit. Two coats hand-rubbed Danish oil. Domino pieces: walnut on one face, maple on the other, ⅛ inch chamfer on all edges. Solution card printed on archival paper, sealed in a wax envelope.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 31 two-sided dominoes: ½ walnut, ½ maple (glued down the center), 3×1½ inches. Stored in a fitted mahogany tray that slides under the board. Optional: brass corner brackets on board frame.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Dominoes arrived in Europe from China around 1750 and the classic double-six set of 28 tiles has been unchanged ever since. Building your own wooden domino set from a single length of hardwood is a satisfying workshop project — each tile gets drilled pip holes, sanded smooth, and finished with oil. The result is a set that will last generations.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One length of 1×2 inch hardwood (oak, cherry, or maple) — about 24 inches long (Home Depot lumber department)|Miter saw or hand saw with miter box|Sandpaper — 80, 120, and 220 grit|Drill and 3/16 inch Forstner bit or brad-point drill bit (for pip holes)|Danish oil or tung oil finish|Dark wood stain or black acrylic paint for pip filling|Pencil and ruler|Clamps|Safety glasses
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Cut the hardwood into 28 identical tiles: each tile 2 inches long × 1 inch wide × ⅜ inch thick (the standard domino ratio is 2:1 length to width). A 24-inch piece of 1×2 lumber will yield exactly 28 tiles if cut to 2-inch lengths. Clamp the wood securely before each cut.|2. Sand all tiles: start with 80-grit to remove saw marks, then 120-grit, then 220-grit for a silky finish. Sand all 6 faces and all edges.|3. Mark the pip positions on each tile with pencil using the standard double-six domino pattern: draw a faint dividing line across the center of each tile (the 'bone line'). Mark pip positions on each half: 0 pips (blank), 1 pip (center), 2 pips (diagonal), 3 pips (diagonal + center), 4 pips (4 corners), 5 pips (4 corners + center), 6 pips (3 pairs). Lay out all 28 tiles per the complete double-6 set (all combinations 0-0 through 6-6).|4. Drill pip holes: use a 3/16 inch drill bit, drilling about ⅛ inch deep. A drill press gives the most consistent depth; a hand drill works with care. Clamp each tile firmly.|5. Apply Danish oil or tung oil to all tiles. Let soak for 15 minutes then wipe off excess. Allow 24 hours to cure. Apply a second coat.|6. Fill drilled pip holes with black acrylic paint using a toothpick. Let dry. Wipe the surface to remove any excess paint, leaving pips dark.|7. Stack and store tiles in a small wooden box or cloth bag.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Adult must supervise or operate the miter saw — keep fingers well clear of the blade, use a stop block to make consistent cuts. A drill press is much safer than a hand drill for young children. Wear safety glasses at all times when cutting and drilling. Oil finish should be applied with good ventilation; oil-soaked rags can spontaneously combust — spread flat to dry outdoors.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Dominoes were likely brought to Europe by Dominican monks or traders from China in the 1700s — the name may derive from the black-and-white masquerade masks called dominos. The double-six set has 28 tiles: 7 tiles for each pip value (0 through 6). The Block Game rule is simple: if you can't play, you pass. First to empty their hand wins. For a premium set, use alternating walnut and maple tiles with inlaid pip dots of contrasting wood. Many Victorian families carved their own sets and stored them in fitted ivory or bone boxes.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: 28-tile set in solid cherry or walnut hardwood, each tile 2×1×⅜ inch. Pip holes drilled with Forstner bit and filled with contrasting wood plugs (maple pips on walnut tiles, or walnut pips on maple). Bone-line divider inlaid or routed.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Each tile: 2 inches × 1 inch × ⅜ inch (standard proportions). Pip holes: 3/16 inch diameter × ⅛ inch deep. Storage box: 6×4×1½ inches.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Tiles sanded to 320 grit. Three coats hand-rubbed Danish oil, buffed between coats. Pip holes filled with contrasting-wood turned pegs (glued flush and sanded). Bone line: routed ⅛ inch wide × 1/16 inch deep, filled with white or ebony wood filler. Storage box: matched wood with sliding lid or brass clasp.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 28 tiles. Optional: brass spinner in center of each tile (traditional style). Storage in a fitted sliding-lid box in matching wood with felt-lined interior.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Draughts — known as Checkers in America — is one of the world's oldest games, with boards found in ancient Egypt. Building a proper 8×8 board from hardwood with painted or inlaid squares is a rewarding workshop project. The finished board doubles as a chess board, making it one of the most useful things a family can build together.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 14×14 inch hardwood board, ¾ inch thick (maple, cherry, or oak from Home Depot lumber or a hardwood dealer)|Alternatively: 14×14 inch birch plywood, ¾ inch thick|Dark and light wood stain (ebony and natural) OR two acrylic paint colors (black and cream)|Painter's tape (1-inch wide)|Matte polyurethane finish|Sandpaper — 80, 120, 220 grit|24 wooden checker discs, 1½ inch diameter (Hobby Lobby or Woodcraft) — 12 in each of two colors|Optional: router and ¼ inch roundover bit for edge treatment|Ruler, pencil, compass or T-square
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Cut or purchase the board blank. Sand with 80-grit, then 120-grit, then 220-grit. Wipe clean.|2. Mark the 8×8 grid: the playing area should be 12 inches × 12 inches (64 squares at 1½ inch each), centered on the board with a 1-inch border on each side. Mark all grid lines with a ruler and pencil.|3. Apply painter's tape along every other column to mask off the light squares in the first row. Paint the exposed squares dark. Repeat for every row, carefully offsetting the pattern to create the checkerboard (dark square in bottom-left corner as viewed by each player — 'dark on the left').|4. Alternatively: apply two contrasting stains, masking with tape between each application.|5. Remove tape carefully. Touch up any bleed-through with a small brush.|6. Apply 2 coats of matte polyurethane. Let cure fully (48 hours) before handling.|7. Optional: route a ¼ inch roundover on all edges of the board for a finished look.|8. Paint or stain 12 checker discs in each of two colors (e.g., red and black). Apply clear coat.|9. Set up the game: each player places 12 pieces on the dark squares of their first 3 rows.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Router use requires adult operation only — a router spinning at 20,000+ RPM will cause serious injury if the workpiece slips. Clamp board firmly. Miter saw and router work require safety glasses and hearing protection. Polyurethane requires ventilation.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Draughts was one of the most widely played games in the Victorian parlor — every household owned a board. The word 'draught' means 'a move' in Middle English. British rules require mandatory captures — if you can jump, you must. American checkers allows kings to move multiple squares in some variations. In 1952, Arthur Samuel's draughts-playing program became the first self-learning AI — trained by playing millions of games against itself. For a premium board, alternate walnut and maple strips in a butcher-block pattern, then crosscut to create a naturally-colored checkerboard without any paint.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Alternating walnut and maple wood strip inlay board, ¾ inch thick, with cherry or walnut border frame. 24 turned hardwood checker discs in walnut and maple.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 16×16 inches. Playing grid: 8×8 squares at 1¾ inch each (14 inch playing area). Border frame: 1 inch wide. Thickness: ¾ inch. Checker discs: 1¾ inch diameter × ¼ inch thick.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Wood-inlay squares sanded flush to 320 grit. Two coats hand-rubbed Danish oil. Border frame with mitered corners, ⅛ inch roundover on top edge. Checker discs: turned, chamfered edge, hand-oiled. Optional: monogram or date laser-engraved on board underside.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 24 turned hardwood checker discs: 12 walnut, 12 maple. Each 1¾ inch diameter, ¼ inch thick, chamfered edge. Stored in two felt bags tied with ribbon. Optional: sliding drawer built into board frame for piece storage.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Chess has been played for over 1,400 years — from its origins in India as Chaturanga through the Arab world to the European courts of the Middle Ages. Building a hand-crafted chessboard is one of the most classic woodworking projects, combining precise measurement, careful painting or inlay, and a satisfying result the family will use for decades. This guide focuses on building the board; standard Staunton chess pieces can be sourced separately.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 18×18 inch birch plywood or hardwood board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Black and cream acrylic paint OR alternating wood stains (Michaels or Home Depot)|Painter's tape, 1-inch wide|Matte polyurethane spray sealant|Sandpaper — 80, 120, 220 grit|Ruler, T-square or carpenter's square, pencil|Optional: router with ⅛ inch roundover bit for edges|Optional: 1×2 inch hardwood strips in two contrasting woods for a true wood-inlay board
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board surface with 80, 120, then 220-grit sandpaper. Wipe clean with a tack cloth.|2. Mark the 8×8 playing grid: USCF tournament standard uses 2¼ inch squares. For an 18-inch board with a 1-inch border, use 2-inch squares (16 inch playing area). Mark all 9 horizontal and 9 vertical lines in pencil with a T-square.|3. Tape off alternating squares using 2-inch-wide painter's tape. Apply tape along full rows, then cut at each vertical line with a craft knife — this gives clean edges. Paint the exposed dark squares first with dark acrylic paint. Apply two coats. Let dry fully.|4. Remove tape, realign for the light squares, and paint with cream or off-white. Let dry.|5. Apply 3 coats of matte polyurethane, allowing full drying between coats. Wet-sand lightly with 400-grit between coats 2 and 3 for an ultra-smooth finish.|6. Optional: rout a ¼ inch roundover on the board's top edge and sand smooth.|7. Label the board coordinates (a-h along one side, 1-8 along the other) with fine-tip marker or stencil paint if desired — useful for learning notation.|8. Set up pieces: white queen on white (d1), black queen on black (d8); light square always on the right for each player.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Router use requires adult supervision only. Always clamp the workpiece firmly and use a featherboard if routing the edges. Wear safety glasses and hearing protection. Polyurethane: ventilate the workspace well and wear a respirator mask.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Chess reached Europe from the Arab world around 900 AD; the modern rules (including the queen's powerful movement) were standardized around 1475. The Staunton piece design — now the international tournament standard — was first sold in 1849 and named after English chess champion Howard Staunton. For a premium board, build the checkerboard pattern using alternating strips of walnut and maple glued into a panel, then crosscut into rows and re-glued with the pattern reversed — the traditional 'strip-and-crosscut' method used by professional board makers. The resulting board has no paint and is far more durable.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Strip-and-crosscut walnut/maple inlay chessboard, ¾ inch thick, with figured walnut or rosewood border frame. Playing squares 2¼ inches. No paint — pure wood color contrast.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 20×20 inches. Playing grid: 8×8 squares at 2¼ inch each (18 inch playing area). Border frame: 1 inch walnut or rosewood. Thickness: ¾ inch. Optional coordinate labels inlaid in frame.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Strip-and-crosscut method: ¼ inch wide alternating strips of walnut and maple glued, sliced, and re-glued in offset pattern. Sanded flush to 320 grit. Three coats hand-rubbed Danish oil, wet-sanded between coats. Frame mitered at corners with spline reinforcement. Bottom felt-lined.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: Board only (pieces sourced separately — recommend Staunton pattern in ebonized boxwood and natural boxwood). Optional: sliding drawer in board frame for storage. Alternatively, we can commission a full turned-wood Staunton set in walnut and maple.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Halma — Greek for 'jump' — was invented in 1883 by Harvard professor George Howard Monks and became an immediate Victorian parlor sensation. Played on a 16×16 grid with pieces racing from corner 'camps' to the opposite corner, it's the direct ancestor of Chinese Checkers. Building the full 16×16 board requires careful measuring but no advanced tools beyond a ruler, tape, and steady hand.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 20×20 inch birch plywood or hardwood board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Cream/ivory and dark green acrylic paint OR alternating wood stains|Painter's tape, ¾ inch wide|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 80, 120, 220 grit|Ruler, T-square, pencil|Red or contrasting paint for corner 'camp' areas|64 wooden craft discs or pawns in 2 or 4 colors, 1 inch diameter (Michaels or Hobby Lobby) — 19 pieces per player for 2-player, 13 for 4-player|Optional: router for edge treatment
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand board smooth. Apply a base coat of cream acrylic paint. Let dry.|2. Mark the 16×16 grid: for a 20-inch board with 1-inch borders, use squares of 1⅛ inch each (18 inches playing area). Mark all 17 horizontal and 17 vertical lines in pencil. Take time — this grid has 256 squares total and accuracy matters.|3. Apply painter's tape along every other row of squares. Paint the exposed squares with dark green paint. Allow to dry. Reapply tape in the alternate pattern and paint those squares. This creates the 16×16 checkerboard.|4. Mark the corner 'camps': the two-player camp is a stepped triangle occupying 19 squares in each corner, in a 1-2-3-4-5-4 stair pattern. The four-player camp is 13 squares in a 1-2-3-4-3 pattern. Paint camp borders with a thick bright line (e.g., red or gold) to clearly delineate each starting zone.|5. Remove all tape. Touch up edges. Apply 3 coats of matte polyurethane.|6. Paint player pieces in distinct colors. For 2 players: 19 pieces each. For 4 players: 13 pieces each. Apply clear coat.|7. Set up: each player fills their corner camp with all their pieces. Players race to move all pieces to the opposite corner.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Spray polyurethane must be used outdoors or in a well-ventilated workshop. Wear a dust mask when sanding the large board surface. Router edge-treatment requires adult operation only with proper clamping.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Halma was invented by George Howard Monks, a Boston surgeon, who may have been inspired by the English game of Hoppity. A smaller 8×8 version evolved into Chinese Checkers in 1892 when the star-shaped board was introduced (confusingly, Chinese Checkers has no Chinese origins). In the original Victorian game, pieces can move in all 8 directions and jump in chains — the key strategy is building 'ladders' of your own pieces that subsequent pieces can hop along in long chains. The 16×16 board also works for draughts variants and other grid games.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Alternating walnut and maple or light/dark veneer inlay on a ¾ inch maple substrate. Corner camp boundaries inlaid with contrasting wood strips or brass rule. 4-color set of wooden cone pawns.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 22×22 inches. Playing grid: 16×16 squares at 1¼ inch each (20 inch playing area). Border: 1 inch walnut frame. Camp boundary: 4 triangular areas marked with inlaid contrasting border. Piece pawns: ½ inch base diameter, ¾ inch tall cones.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Inlay squares sanded flush to 320 grit. Two coats hand-rubbed Danish oil. Camp zones highlighted with ⅛ inch inlaid maple or ebony rule. Border frame mitered corners. Board underside felt-lined. Optional: player's initials laser-engraved at each camp.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: For 2-player: 38 turned wooden cones (19 each in 2 contrasting wood types). For 4-player: 52 turned wooden cones (13 each in 4 colors: natural maple, walnut, cherry, ebonized oak). Stored in a fitted divided tray.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: International Draughts — played on a 10×10 board with 20 pieces per player — is the official game of the World Draughts Championships and considerably more complex than the standard 8×8 version. Turkish Draughts uses an 8×8 board but pieces move and capture orthogonally rather than diagonally. Building a 10×10 board opens up a whole family of draughts variants to explore.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 18×18 inch birch plywood or hardwood board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Black and cream acrylic paint OR alternating wood stains|Painter's tape, ¾ inch wide|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 80, 120, 220 grit|Ruler, T-square, pencil|40 wooden checker discs, 1¼ inch diameter — 20 in each of two colors (Hobby Lobby or Woodcraft)
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Apply cream base coat. Let dry.|2. Mark a 10×10 grid: for an 18-inch board with 1-inch borders, use squares of 1.6 inch each (16 inch playing area). Alternatively use 1½ inch squares (15 inch playing area with 1.5 inch borders). Mark all 11 horizontal and 11 vertical grid lines with T-square and pencil.|3. Tape and paint alternating squares dark (black or dark brown). Allow to dry fully. The playing color is dark squares only — per International Draughts convention, the board is oriented so a dark square is in the bottom-left corner for each player.|4. Remove tape, touch up, apply 3 coats of polyurethane.|5. Paint 20 checker discs in each of two colors. Apply clear coat.|6. Set up for International Draughts: each player places 20 pieces on dark squares of the first 4 rows. The two middle rows are empty to start.|7. Write game rules on a reference card for both International Draughts and Turkish Draughts variants.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Spray paint and polyurethane require outdoor use or strong ventilation. Wear dust mask when sanding large board areas. Adult supervision for any cutting tools.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: International Draughts (also called Polish Draughts) was standardized in the Netherlands in the early 18th century and now has governing bodies in over 50 countries. Key differences from English draughts: men can capture backwards, and kings ('dames') can move any number of squares diagonally like a bishop in chess. The 10×10 board creates a far deeper game — grandmaster-level play requires memory of specific endgame positions. The board you build also works perfectly for standard English draughts (using only the inner 8×8 grid) and Turkish Draughts.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Alternating walnut and maple inlay 10×10 board, ¾ inch thick, with walnut border frame. Optional engraved coordinate labels for tournament use. 40 turned hardwood checker discs.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 18×18 inches. Playing grid: 10×10 squares at 1½ inch each (15 inch playing area). Border: 1½ inch walnut frame. Thickness: ¾ inch. Checker discs: 1¼ inch diameter × ¼ inch thick.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Inlay squares sanded flush to 320 grit. Hand-rubbed Danish oil. Border frame mitered corners with spline reinforcement. Felt underside. Optional: coordinate numbers engraved per International Draughts notation (1-50 on dark squares).
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 40 turned hardwood checker discs: 20 walnut, 20 maple. Each 1¼ inch diameter × ¼ inch thick with chamfered edges. Stored in a fitted box. Note: checker discs should be 75-80% of square size so they fit comfortably.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Reversi was invented in England in 1883 and re-launched as Othello in 1971 with the tagline 'a minute to learn, a lifetime to master.' The game uses 64 two-sided discs on an 8×8 board — discs flip to show your color when flanked by an opponent. Building the board and two-toned disc set from wood creates a beautiful heirloom version of this fast-playing strategy game.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 12×12 inch birch plywood or pine board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Dark green acrylic paint for board surface (Michaels)|Black acrylic paint for grid lines|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|64 wooden craft discs, 1 inch diameter (Michaels or Hobby Lobby) — paint 32 black on one side, white on the other|Ruler, pencil, thin brush|Optional: ½ inch drill and 1-inch Forstner bit if making recessed disc wells
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Paint the entire surface dark green. Let dry, apply second coat.|2. Mark the 8×8 grid: for a 12-inch board, use 1¼ inch squares (10 inch playing area with 1-inch border). Mark 9 horizontal and 9 vertical lines in pencil.|3. Paint thin black lines along all grid lines with a fine brush and ruler. Alternatively, use a black fine-tip permanent marker and straight-edge.|4. Add the four center-dot markers (traditional Othello boards have small dots at the four intersections that frame the center 4 squares): mark positions at d4/d5/e4/e5 intersections.|5. Apply 2 coats of matte polyurethane over the whole board. Let cure.|6. Make the two-sided discs: paint one face of all 64 discs black, the other white (or natural wood). Let dry, seal with a clear coat. Alternatively, glue two differently colored craft discs back-to-back.|7. Set up: place 2 black and 2 white discs in the center 4 squares in a diagonal pattern (white at d4 and e5, black at d5 and e4 — or any diagonal arrangement agreed upon).|8. Play begins with black moving first. Each turn, place a disc so it flanks at least one opponent's disc in a straight line; flip all flanked discs to your color.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Polyurethane and spray paint require well-ventilated area. Sand with a dust mask. If using a drill for disc wells, adult supervision required.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Reversi was independently invented by Lewis Waterman and John Mollett in 1883 — both claimed credit and a dispute lasted years. Mattel's 1971 re-release as Othello was enormously successful. World Othello Championships have been held annually since 1977. A key strategic concept: corner squares are the most valuable positions since they can never be flanked. For a professional-quality build, use a green-felt-covered board and drill shallow 1-inch diameter × ⅛-inch-deep wells for each square so discs sit recessed and don't slide — this is how competition sets are built.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Dark green leather or felt playing surface mounted on ½ inch MDF or maple substrate, with walnut or cherry hardwood border frame. 64 two-sided discs in ebonized maple (black side) and natural maple (white side).
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Overall board: 14×14 inches. Playing grid: 8×8 squares at 1½ inch each (12 inch playing area). Border frame: 1 inch walnut. Thickness: ¾ inch. Disc diameter: 1⅜ inch. Recessed wells: ⅛ inch deep.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Border frame walnut, hand-oiled, mitered corners. Playing surface: green baize or leather fitted inside frame, secured with contact cement. Grid lines: white or gold silk-screened on felt. Disc wells: 64 recessed circles ⅛ inch deep, routed with 1⅜ inch Forstner bit. Discs: lathe-turned, chamfered, 1 face ebonized, 1 face natural maple.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 64 turned maple discs, 1⅜ inch diameter × ⅜ inch thick. One face ebonized (Japan black or black dye), other face natural maple oil finish. Chamfered edges. Stored in two felt pouches inside a sliding-lid box built into board base.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Noughts and Crosses (Tic-Tac-Toe) Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: Noughts and Crosses — Tic-Tac-Toe in America — has been traced to ancient Rome and is arguably the oldest pencil-and-paper game still played. A permanent wooden version makes a charming, reusable game for all ages. This project uses simple materials: a wood square, wood-burned or painted lines, and smooth river stones or carved discs as playing pieces.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 8×8 inch pine or birch board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot) OR a 9×9 inch wooden craft plaque (Michaels)|Acrylic paint — cream or white for the board, dark brown or black for grid lines|Fine-tip brush|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|10 playing pieces — 5 each of two types: smooth river stones (painted X and O), or 1-inch wooden discs (Michaels) labeled X and O with paint or marker|Optional: wood-burning pen for burned-in grid lines
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Apply a coat of cream or white acrylic paint. Let dry. Apply a second coat.|2. Mark the 3×3 grid: draw two horizontal lines and two vertical lines to divide the board into 9 equal squares. For an 8-inch board, lines at 2⅔ inches and 5⅓ inches from each edge.|3. Paint or burn the four grid lines neatly. For painted lines: use a fine-tip brush and a ruler as a guide. For burned lines: use a wood-burning pen and a metal ruler — burns in about 30 seconds per line. Make lines about ⅛ inch wide.|4. Apply 2 coats of matte polyurethane over the painted board. Let cure.|5. Make playing pieces: paint 5 craft discs with large X symbols (or letter X), and 5 with O symbols. Use contrasting paint colors. Apply clear coat. Alternatively, collect 10 smooth pebbles and paint X or O on each.|6. Optionally: add a small storage pouch or attached felt bag to keep pieces with the board.|7. Play: two players alternate placing their symbol in any empty square. First to get three in a row (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal) wins.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Wood-burning pen tip is extremely hot — adult operation only. Never leave unattended. Polyurethane spray requires ventilation.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: The Roman version of Tic-Tac-Toe, called Terni Lapilli, was played on boards scratched into stone — graffiti examples survive in Pompeii. Unlike modern Tic-Tac-Toe, players could move their pieces rather than adding new ones, which makes the Roman version more strategically rich. A beautifully built wooden set — with smooth stones as X's and O's — makes a genuine heirloom from the simplest possible game. For an upgrade, use a natural wood-slice board (a cross-section of a branch) and burn the grid lines in for a rustic look.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Natural wood-slice board (cross-section of a branch, 8-10 inch diameter), live edge, hand-sanded and oiled. Grid lines wood-burned. Playing pieces: smooth river stones or turned walnut and maple discs.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 8–10 inch diameter wood slice, ¾ inch thick. Grid: two burned lines in each direction, dividing board into 9 equal zones. Pieces: 5 smooth stones per player (2-3 inch stones), or 5 turned discs per player (1½ inch diameter × ½ inch thick).
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Live-edge board: bark edge preserved or cleaned, natural oil finish (2-3 coats Danish oil). Grid lines: wood-burned ⅛ inch wide. Stone pieces: selected, sanded smooth if needed, one set painted with X (white paint), one set painted with O (black paint), sealed with clear coat. Alternatively, turned wood discs in contrasting walnut and maple.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 10 smooth river stones or turned hardwood discs, matched in size. 5 labeled X, 5 labeled O. Stored in a small cloth bag attached to board with a jute cord.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Fox and Geese Peg Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: This version of Fox and Geese uses a drilled peg board — the same cross-shaped layout with 33 holes as the classic English Peg Solitaire board. Peg-style playing pieces slot into the holes, giving the game a satisfying tactile quality and a classic Victorian look. The cross-shaped board with peg pieces was how Fox and Geese was commonly sold in Victorian toy shops.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 9×9 inch pine or birch board, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Drill and ¼ inch drill bit (or brad-point bit)|One 36-inch length of ¼ inch wooden dowel (Home Depot)|Sandpaper — 80, 120, 220 grit|Dark stain or paint for board|Danish oil or polyurethane sealant|Ruler, pencil|Handsaw or miter saw for cutting pegs|Red acrylic paint for fox peg
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Apply dark wood stain or paint. Let dry.|2. Mark the 33 hole positions: the cross-shaped board is a 7×7 grid of points with the four corner 2×2 groups removed. For a 9-inch board, mark a 7×7 grid of dots at 1¼ inch spacing, centered (with ⅞ inch border). Erase the 16 corner dots (4 dots in each of the 4 corners) — 49 − 16 = 33 remaining positions.|3. Drill ¼ inch holes at each of the 33 marked positions, approximately ½ inch deep. A drill press gives the most consistent depth; clamp the board firmly if using a hand drill.|4. Add diagonal line markings: in each of the 4 arms of the cross, draw diagonal lines connecting adjacent holes — these diagonals are part of the Fox and Geese movement rules. Use paint or a wood-burning pen.|5. Draw/paint horizontal and vertical connecting lines between all adjacent holes.|6. Apply polyurethane sealant over the painted board (around the holes). Let cure.|7. Cut 14 pegs from ¼ inch dowel: each peg should be ¾ to 1 inch long (about 1.5× the board thickness). Sand all pegs smooth.|8. Paint 13 pegs white or cream (geese) and 1 peg red or orange (fox). Let dry. Seal with clear coat.|9. Set up: place the 13 geese pegs in all 33 holes of the bottom three rows. Fox peg starts in any unoccupied position in the upper portion.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Drill use requires adult supervision. Use a drill press if available — it prevents the bit from wandering. Clamp the board securely before drilling. Wear safety glasses. Miter saw or handsaw for peg cutting requires adult operation.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Fox and Geese appears in a 1470 inventory of King Edward IV of England, and versions of the game have been found carved in medieval Scandinavian stonework (as Halatafl). The peg-board version was standard in Victorian toy shops and is still sold today. The fox peg can capture a goose by jumping over it to an empty hole — so the fox aims to reduce the geese, while geese aim to surround the fox. The 33-hole board is identical to a standard English Peg Solitaire board, so the same build gives you two games.

fabrica_spec.commission_title: Fox and Geese (Peg Board) — Artisan Commission
fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Walnut or cherry hardwood board, ¾ inch thick, with 33 drilled holes. Turned wooden pegs in two contrasting woods. Board optionally shaped to the cross outline (outer edge routed to the cross shape).
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 9×9 inches (square) or cross-shaped cutout. 33 holes in cross pattern: 7×7 grid minus 4 corner 2×2 groups, at 1¼ inch spacing. Hole diameter: ¼ inch, ½ inch deep. Pegs: ¼ inch diameter dowel, ¾ inch tall above surface.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Board sanded to 320 grit, hand-oiled with 2 coats Danish oil. Lines between holes engraved or burned. Cross outline edge optionally routed with ¼ inch roundover. Pegs turned to a mushroom-cap head for easier grip.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 14 turned wood pegs: 13 maple or ash (geese), 1 cherry or rosewood (fox). Each ¼ inch diameter, ¾ inch above surface, with ¼ inch peg that slots into hole. Stored in a small drilled storage strip on board underside.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Chuck-a-Luck was a staple of American frontier gambling — three dice tumble in a birdcage, and players bet on which numbers appear. The betting layout is a colorful numbered cloth or board showing numbers 1-6 with their payouts. Building a family-friendly parlor version with craft materials creates a Victorian-style probability teaching tool as much as a game.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One piece of green felt, 18×12 inches (Hobby Lobby or Joann) OR one 18×12 inch plywood board painted green|6 colored number tiles or painted wooden blocks (numbers 1-6)|Fabric paint or acrylic paint in red, gold, and white (Michaels)|Ruler and pencil|Fabric marker or stencils for numbers|3 standard six-sided dice|A lidded plastic container or a 3-inch diameter cup with lid (for rolling 3 dice — simulates the birdcage)|Clear acrylic sealer (if making a board version)|Scissors or craft knife
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Lay out the felt or board. Mark 6 rectangular betting zones, each 3×4 inches, arranged in a row. Leave 2 inches at the top and bottom for border decoration.|2. Paint or mark each zone with a large number 1 through 6. Use large, bold numerals centered in each zone. For felt, use fabric paint; for wood, use acrylic paint.|3. Add payout information below each number: '1 die: 1× | 2 dice: 2× | 3 dice: 3×' — write this clearly in small text within each zone.|4. Add a decorative border in gold paint around the layout.|5. Optional: add a 'House wins' note at the top: 'If your number doesn't appear, your bet is lost.'|6. For felt: iron flat when dry. For board version: apply 2 coats of matte polyurethane to protect paint.|7. Create a dice cup: use a lidded plastic container. Place 3 dice inside, snap lid shut, and shake — lift lid over the layout to reveal results.|8. Create simple betting tokens from small craft discs or cardboard chips. Each player starts with 10-20 tokens.|9. Play: before rolling, each player places tokens on any number zone. Shake dice cup and reveal results. Pay out per payout table.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Craft knife use requires adult supervision. Spray sealant must be used outdoors. This is a probability game — use tokens or play money only, not real money.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Chuck-a-Luck (also called Sweat-Cloth or Bird Cage) was a fixture of American frontier saloons and was strongly associated with the Wild West era. The distinctive birdcage tumbler — a wire cage on an axle that flips end over end to mix 3 dice — became the game's iconic image. The house advantage is about 7.9% in the standard payout version. This makes it an excellent family probability lesson: play a 'fair' version (single die payout = 1×, double = 3×, triple = 5×) and compare results. The Victorian household version used actual dice in a cloth-covered cup rather than the birdcage, making it easy to recreate at home.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Green baize playing surface on a ¾ inch MDF or hardwood substrate, 18×12 inches. Six numbered zones silk-screened or painted in gilt on green. Brass birdcage tumbler with 3 engraved dice. Hardwood frame.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Layout: 18×12 inches. Six betting zones each 2½×4 inches in a row. Border: 1 inch hardwood or leather frame. Optional cage: 4-inch diameter wire birdcage tumbler on a walnut stand.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Baize surface fitted over substrate, tacked under frame. Numbers in gold silk-screen or hand-painted gilt. Frame in stained walnut or mahogany with brass corner pieces. Cage: brass wire or copper-plated wire frame on turned walnut axle stand.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 3 large dice (1 inch), engraved with standard pip pattern. 60 betting tokens: antique-finish brass discs, ¾ inch diameter. Optional: full Victorian birdcage tumbler in brass on a walnut stand.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Postal Chess — correspondence chess by mail — was enormously popular in the Victorian era, with games lasting weeks or months as moves were exchanged by post. Building a dedicated correspondence chess set means creating a beautiful notation card system and a compact travel board for tracking your game. This is a project that combines woodcraft, calligraphy, and the slow pleasures of a long-distance intellectual contest.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 10×10 inch birch plywood board, ¼–½ inch thick (Home Depot)|Black and cream acrylic paint for chessboard|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|Ruler, pencil, T-square|Permanent marker or stencils for coordinate labels (a-h, 1-8)|Heavy cream cardstock for move notation cards (Michaels)|Envelopes (if playing by physical mail)|Optional: set of small magnetic chess pieces (available online or at game stores) for a travel-sized board|Fine-tip pen for writing notation
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand and paint the chessboard (8×8 grid) on the plywood: for a 10-inch board with ½ inch border, mark 2-inch squares (8×2 = 16 inch playing area — too large; use 1-inch squares for a compact correspondence board with a 1-inch border). Mark 9 horizontal and 9 vertical lines, paint alternating squares.|2. Apply polyurethane sealant. Let cure.|3. Label the board coordinates: letters a-h along the bottom edge (white's side), numbers 1-8 along the left edge. Use small stencils or careful freehand lettering.|4. Create a set of notation cards: cut cardstock into 5×3 inch cards. Rule each card with columns for 'Move #', 'White', 'Black', 'Date Sent'. Print or hand-write 30 rows per card.|5. Decorate cards with a simple border or monogram. Seal completed cards with a light laminate or clear tape over the front.|6. Write an instruction sheet explaining algebraic notation: piece letters (K=King, Q=Queen, R=Rook, B=Bishop, N=Knight), square coordinates (e.g. e2-e4 or Ng1-f3), and special moves (O-O = kingside castle, + = check, # = checkmate).|7. Create a small storage folder or box for the cards, instruction sheet, and envelopes.|8. Optional: attach magnetic chess pieces to the board so they stay in place between moves.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Polyurethane spray requires ventilation. No sharp tools required for the card-making portion — safe for all ages with adult guidance on notation system.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: The first organized correspondence chess tournament was held in 1824 in Britain, and postal chess clubs flourished throughout the Victorian era. Players might have 10-20 ongoing games simultaneously, sending a postcard per move. Today, correspondence chess continues online (ICCF — International Correspondence Chess Federation). The historical appeal is the pace: having a week to think over your move, consulting books, and writing a careful notation card. Building a beautiful correspondence set with quality card stock and a personal notation system is a tribute to this contemplative tradition.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Compact folding chess board in walnut and maple inlay, ½ inch thick when folded. Letterpress-printed notation cards on cream laid stock. Optional: magnetic travel chess pieces.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Folding board: 8×8 inches unfolded (4×8 inches folded). 8×8 playing grid at 1-inch squares. Notation cards: 5×3 inches, 30-row format, 20 cards per set. Storage: fitted leather or book-cloth portfolio.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Folding board: piano hinge in brass, walnut frame, inlay squares sanded flush. Hand-rubbed oil finish. Coordinate labels inlaid or laser-engraved in frame. Notation cards: letterpress or laser-printed on 100lb cream cardstock with monogram or family crest option.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: Optional: 32 magnetic Staunton-style chessmen in turned boxwood (natural and ebonized), with magnetic bases. Stored in a velvet-lined tray inside the folded board. Portfolio: bookcloth-covered, with pocket for cards and envelopes.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build Lewis Carroll's Game of Logic Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: Lewis Carroll published The Game of Logic in 1887 as a way to teach syllogistic reasoning through a simple diagram and colored counters. The biliteral diagram is a 2×2 grid, and the triliteral diagram adds an inner square — together they represent logical categories and propositions. Building the boards and counter set makes Carroll's elegant logic system fully playable.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: Two pieces of stiff cardstock or thin cardboard, each 6×6 inches (Michaels or craft supply)|Ruler and pencil|Black fine-tip marker|Red acrylic paint and grey acrylic paint (Michaels)|9 small wooden craft discs or thick card circles, ¾ inch diameter — 4 red and 5 grey (Michaels)|Scissors or craft knife|Optional: lamination pouches for durability|Clear acrylic spray sealant
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Create the Biliteral Diagram (the simpler 2×2 grid): on a 4×4 inch area of the first cardstock, draw a large square divided into 4 equal squares (2 columns, 2 rows). Label the left column 'x' (top) and 'x'' (bottom), the right column 'y' (top-left quadrant) and 'y'' (top-right quadrant). Note: x = has property, x' = does not have property.|2. Draw the outer boundary of the Biliteral diagram clearly. Label it neatly.|3. Create the Triliteral Diagram: on the second cardstock, draw an 8-square grid — a 2×4 arrangement representing the addition of an 'm' attribute. An inner rectangle within each of the four squares represents 'm' (has middle property), the outer area represents 'm'' (does not). This gives 8 cells total.|4. Label the Triliteral diagram regions: top half = x, bottom half = x', left half = y, right half = y'. Inner cells = m, outer cells = m'.|5. Go over all pencil lines with black marker. Make the diagrams bold and clear.|6. Seal both diagrams with clear acrylic spray or laminate.|7. Paint 4 craft discs red (meaning 'at least one exists here') and 5 discs grey (meaning 'nothing exists here'). Let dry. Seal with clear coat.|8. Print or hand-write a short rule card: Red counter in a cell = 'something with these properties exists.' Grey counter in a cell = 'nothing with these properties exists.' Half-in-half position = 'something may exist in one of these two cells but we don't know which.'|9. Store diagrams and counters in a small envelope or tin.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Craft knife for cutting cardstock requires adult supervision. Spray sealant requires ventilation. This is primarily a non-tool project — safe for younger children with adult guidance on the logic rules.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Lewis Carroll wrote The Game of Logic in 1887 — four years before the symbolic logic now taught in universities was standardized by Frege and Russell. His counter-based diagrammatic system was an independent invention, and Carroll claimed it was easier for beginners than symbolic notation. Carroll's syllogisms are often whimsical: 'All cats understand French | Some chickens are cats / Therefore: some chickens understand French.' The diagrams work as Venn diagrams before Venn's publication — a genuine historical artifact of Victorian recreational mathematics. The counter set can be used with the puzzles in the original 1887 book, which is freely available on Project Gutenberg.

fabrica_spec.commission_title: The Game of Logic — Artisan Commission
fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Two inlaid hardwood diagram boards (biliteral and triliteral), each 5×5 inches, in contrasting wood with routed cell divisions. 9 turned hardwood counters in red-stained and grey-stained maple.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Biliteral board: 5×5 inches, 2×2 grid with 2-inch cells. Triliteral board: 5×5 inches, 8-cell grid (2×4 zones with inner squares). Board thickness: ½ inch. Counters: ¾ inch diameter × ¼ inch thick.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Boards in light maple with routed ⅛ inch wide cell division lines filled with dark stain. Labels engraved (x, x', y, y', m, m'). Hand-rubbed oil finish. Counters: 4 dyed red, 5 dyed grey, all sealed with clear lacquer. Stored in a felt-lined box with a copy of Carroll's original puzzle card set.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 9 turned maple counters: 4 red (¾ inch diameter), 5 grey (¾ inch diameter). Two diagram boards. Puzzle cards: 20 Carroll syllogisms printed on cream cardstock, stored in a silk envelope. Optional: facsimile of the 1887 first edition included.

heritage_skills_entry.summary: Agon is believed to be the oldest board game played on a hexagonal grid — patented in France in 1842 and one of the first games specifically designed for women's drawing rooms. Played on 91 hexagonal spaces arranged in 6 concentric rings, each player races a Queen and 6 Guards toward the central hexagon. Building a hexagonal board requires either careful drafting or a laser cutter — this guide covers both approaches.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: For the drawn/painted approach: One 16×16 inch birch plywood sheet, ¾ inch thick (Home Depot)|Compass and ruler for drawing hexagons|Acrylic paint in 6 distinct ring colors (traditional: 6 concentric rings each a different color — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple)|Fine-tip brush|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|14 wooden craft discs or pawns — 7 per player in 2 colors (Michaels or Hobby Lobby) — 1 queen piece (larger disc) + 6 guards per player
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the plywood smooth. Paint with a light cream base coat. Let dry.|2. Find the center of the board. The Agon board is a hexagonal grid of 91 spaces arranged in concentric rings of 6 hexagons each: Ring 1 = center hex (1 space), Ring 2 = 6 hexes, Ring 3 = 12 hexes, Ring 4 = 18 hexes, Ring 5 = 24 hexes, Ring 6 (outer) = 30 hexes — total 91 hexes.|3. Determine your hex size: for a flat-to-flat distance of 1¼ inch per hexagon, the overall board spans about 7 hexes across the widest point = 8¾ inches. A 12-inch flat-to-flat board uses 1¾ inch hexes. Mark the center point.|4. Use a compass set to your hex radius to step off hexagon centers, starting from the center and working outward ring by ring. Mark all 91 hex centers.|5. Draw each hexagon by setting your compass to the same radius and stepping around each center point 6 times to mark the 6 vertices, then connecting them.|6. Paint each ring in a distinct color: innermost ring 1 = dark purple/indigo, working outward through the spectrum. Traditional Agon boards use 6 distinguishable colors for the 6 rings.|7. Outline each hexagon in black with a fine brush.|8. Apply 2 coats of matte polyurethane. Let cure.|9. Make pieces: 1 queen peg (larger, 1¼ inch disc) and 6 guard pegs (1 inch discs) per player. Paint in 2 contrasting colors. Optional: queen disc is taller or more ornate.|10. Set up: pieces start on the outer ring. Each player arranges their queen and 6 guards alternating with the opponent's pieces around the outer ring, per the starting diagram.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Compass point is sharp — supervise younger children. Router use for board shaping (to hexagonal outline) requires adult-only operation. Spray sealant in ventilated area.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Agon was patented in France in 1842 and may be the oldest game designed specifically on a hexagonal grid. It was considered an elegant drawing-room game for educated women — a deliberate contrast to the military-strategy games like chess that dominated men's gaming. The six concentric color rings are an important navigational feature: players quickly learn to say 'my queen is on the third ring' rather than counting hexagons. The goal — place your queen in the center with guards surrounding it — is deceptively simple. The full book of rules is brief; the strategic depth comes from blocking and maneuvering in the tight central rings.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: CNC-routed or laser-cut hardwood hexagonal board, 12 inches flat-to-flat, ¾ inch thick, mounted in a circular walnut frame. Six concentric rings painted in traditional spectrum colors or stained in natural wood tones. 14 turned hardwood pieces.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board: 12 inches flat-to-flat (13.9 inches corner to corner), ¾ inch thick. 91 hexagonal spaces at 1¾ inch flat-to-flat each. 6 concentric rings. Border: 1 inch walnut ring frame. Queen pieces: 1¼ inch diameter disc, ½ inch tall. Guard pieces: 1 inch diameter disc, ⅜ inch tall.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Hexagonal cells routed ⅛ inch deep or silk-screened with color fills. Ring colors traditional (6-spectrum) or in natural wood tones (ebony, walnut, cherry, maple, ash, bleached). Board sanded to 320 grit, hand-oiled. Circular walnut frame with hand-cut joints. Pieces turned, chamfered, hand-oiled in two contrasting woods.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 14 turned hardwood pieces: 2 queen discs (1¼ inch, ½ inch tall, in contrasting woods or with crown motif), 12 guard discs (1 inch, ⅜ inch tall). Stored in a felt-lined divided box. Optional: laser-engraved crown symbol on queen pieces.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Merelles (Three Men's Morris) Board Set
heritage_skills_entry.summary: Merelles is the family of games that includes Three Men's Morris, Six Men's Morris, Nine Men's Morris, and Twelve Men's Morris — all using nested square boards with connecting lines. The Sociable version from the 1858 sourcebook focuses on the classic Nine Men's Morris with sociable Victorian parlor rules. Building a set of two or three boards in nested sizes lets the whole family play all variants from a single workshop session.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: Three birch plywood squares: 10×10 inch, 8×8 inch, and 6×6 inch — all ¼–½ inch thick (Home Depot or Hobby Lobby)|Acrylic paint: cream/tan for backgrounds, dark brown/black for lines|Fine-tip brushes|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|36 wooden craft discs total: 9 each of 4 different colors, ¾ inch diameter (Michaels)|Ruler, pencil|Optional: wood-burning pen
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand all three boards smooth. Apply cream base coat to each. Let dry.|2. Board 1 (Three Men's Morris — 6×6 inch board): draw one square (5×5 inches centered), then a smaller square inside (2½×2½ inches). Add connecting lines from each midpoint of the outer square to the corresponding midpoint of the inner square. Mark 12 intersection points.|3. Board 2 (Six Men's Morris — 8×8 inch board): draw two concentric squares (7×7 and 3½×3½). Add midpoint connectors. Mark 16 intersection points.|4. Board 3 (Nine Men's Morris — 10×10 inch board): draw three concentric squares (8×8, 5×5, 2×2). Add midpoint connectors. Mark 24 intersection points. (This is the standard Nine Men's Morris board.)|5. Paint all board lines and point marks dark brown or black. Let dry. Seal all boards with polyurethane. Let cure.|6. Paint playing pieces: 9 discs each in 4 different colors (for a 4-player variant, or 9 each in 2 colors for standard).|7. Write a rules card for each board: Three Men's Morris (3 pieces each, only placement — no movement), Six Men's Morris (6 pieces each, same mill rules), Nine Men's Morris (9 pieces, 3 phases).|8. Store all boards and pieces in a flat box or linen bag.
heritage_skills_entry.safety_notes: Spray polyurethane requires ventilation. Wood-burning pen requires adult operation only. Sandpaper creates dust — wear a mask.
heritage_skills_entry.tips_and_context: Merelles boards have been found cut into the benches of Westminster Abbey, the cloister of Canterbury Cathedral, and ancient temple floors in Egypt. Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream mentions the 'nine men's morris' as an overgrown field feature — the game was so common that men played it by scratching boards into any flat surface. The Sociable version in the 1858 source adds a '4-player sociable' variant where two pairs of partners play on opposite sides of a single board. Building three board sizes at once lets you scale the game's complexity for players of different ages.

fabrica_spec.materials_spec: Three-board nested set in walnut, cherry, and maple respectively — one board per Morris variant. Each board with routed or inlaid connecting lines. 36 turned wooden playing pieces in 4 contrasting woods.
fabrica_spec.dimensions: Board 1 (Three Men's Morris): 7×7 inches, 2-square grid. Board 2 (Six Men's Morris): 9×9 inches, 2-square grid. Board 3 (Nine Men's Morris): 11×11 inches, 3-square grid. All boards ¾ inch thick. Pieces: ¾ inch diameter discs, ¼ inch thick, 9 each in 4 woods.
fabrica_spec.finish_notes: Each board in a distinct wood species. Lines routed ⅛ inch wide × 1/16 inch deep, filled with contrasting dark filler. Point positions marked with ⅛ inch brass inlay dots. Boards hand-oiled. Pieces in 4 contrasting woods: walnut, maple, cherry, ebonized oak.
fabrica_spec.pieces_spec: 36 turned discs: 9 each in walnut, maple, cherry, ebonized oak. Stored in a 4-compartment felt-lined tray. Three boards stored flat in a fitted cloth envelope or thin wooden case.

heritage_skills_entry.app_title: Build a Knight's Tour Puzzle Board
heritage_skills_entry.summary: The Knight's Tour — studied by Euler in 1759 and by Arab mathematicians in 840 AD — asks: can a chess knight visit every square of the 8×8 board exactly once? Building a numbered peg board to trace knight moves makes this classic mathematical puzzle tactile and visual. Players place numbered tokens as they move, revealing the tour as a winding path across the board.
heritage_skills_entry.materials_list: One 14×14 inch birch plywood board, ½ inch thick (Home Depot)|Alternating black and cream acrylic paint for chessboard pattern|Matte polyurethane sealant|Sandpaper — 120 and 220 grit|64 small wooden numbered tokens (1-64) OR 64 blank tokens + permanent marker for numbering (Michaels)|Ruler, T-square, pencil|One chess knight piece (from any chess set) as the moving piece|Small wooden cuphooks or pins (optional — as markers for the path)
heritage_skills_entry.steps: 1. Sand the board smooth. Mark and paint an 8×8 chessboard: for a 14-inch board with 1-inch border, use 1½ inch squares (12 inch playing area). Tape and paint alternating squares cream and dark brown.|2. Apply 2 coats of polyurethane sealant. Let cure.|3.
